assessment_items.question_content:
**This diptych was made for Richard II, King of England, who is pictured in the left interior panel and shown kneeling before the Virgin Mary on the right panel.**


![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c0d9e436739068cd9495a2705d1c701c.png)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
**This is correct. Richard II is the patron of the diptych and he is shown kneeling on the left panel before the Virgin Mary on the right panel.**

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/7956a12f418e5650ea88c50667beb4a9.png)

**[Watch this video to review](/humanities/art-history/art-history-1400-1500-renaissance-in-italy-and-the-north/england/v/wilton-diptych)**

assessment_items.question_content:
**Who are the three men who stand behind Richard?**

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9c9fcb2d76958d23303d6ab905001a98.png)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
**The saints are, from right to left, St. John the Baptist holding a lamb, St. Edward the Confessor holding a ring, and St. Edmund holding an arrow. Edward and Edmund were also English kings who were remembered as pious Christians.**

**[Watch this video to review](/humanities/art-history/art-history-1400-1500-renaissance-in-italy-and-the-north/england/v/wilton-diptych)**

assessment_items.question_content:
**On the right, an angel holds the banner of [[☃ dropdown 1]], the patron saint of England.**

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9c9fcb2d76958d23303d6ab905001a98.png)
hints:
**St. George is the patron saint of England.**

**[Watch this video to review](/humanities/art-history/art-history-1400-1500-renaissance-in-italy-and-the-north/england/v/wilton-diptych)**

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following is NOT one of the messages of the Wilton Diptych?**

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/38618ba90702cdd884be384c15caa94e.png)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
**Richard receives both religious and political authority from the Virgin and Child.**

**[Watch this video to review](/humanities/art-history/art-history-1400-1500-renaissance-in-italy-and-the-north/england/v/wilton-diptych)**

choices.content: Richard aspires to the piety of the saintly English kings before him.
Richard receives his divine right to rule from the Virgin and Child.
The angels, who all wear Richard's symbol of the stag, support his right to rule.
Richard's authority to rule is in danger and he is in need of divine protection.
